## Security & Compliance

> ⚠️ LOCKED — These rules cannot be overridden.

### Injection Prevention

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### Parameterized queries only
NEVER concatenate user input into SQL/NoSQL/LDAP queries. ALWAYS use parameterized queries (`$1`, `?`, `:param`), prepared statements, or ORM bindings. Flag: `query("SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = " + id)`, f-strings in SQL, `${variable}` in queries. Use instead: `query("SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = $1", [id])`.

### Data Privacy & PII

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### Never disable TLS/SSL verification
NEVER set `rejectUnauthorized: false`, `verify=False`, `InsecureSkipVerify: true`, or `NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=0` in HTTP clients, database connections, or API calls. Flag as CRITICAL if found. Use proper certificate chains; for local dev, use mkcert to create locally-trusted certificates instead.

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### Encrypt sensitive data at rest and in transit
MUST use TLS 1.2+ for all network communication and AES-256+ for data at rest. NEVER store passwords in plaintext or use MD5/SHA for hashing; ALWAYS use bcrypt, scrypt, or argon2. NEVER implement custom crypto—use only established libraries (crypto, libsodium, openssl).

### Authorization & Access Control

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### Authorization on every endpoint
MUST verify authenticated caller has permission for the specific resource and operation. Every GET/PUT/DELETE /:id route MUST check ownership or role before executing. NEVER rely on client-side guards alone. Example: Check `if (user.id !== resource.ownerId && !user.isAdmin) return 403` on object routes.

### Destructive Operations

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### No unguarded destructive operations
NEVER execute DELETE, DROP, rm -rf, git push --force, or git reset --hard without explicit user confirmation. MUST implement soft deletes, provide rollback scripts for migrations, and require confirmation flags for all destructive operations. Example: Use UPDATE is_deleted=true instead of DELETE.
## Architecture & Patterns

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
### Use designated framework
MUST use the designated framework for all new code and AI-generated output. NEVER introduce alternative frameworks without architecture review. Example: Use Next.js for all React projects, not Remix or Astro.

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
### Code organization standards
MUST organize code into logical modules by feature/domain. Use single-responsibility principle: one class/function per concern. Example: place user authentication in auth/ module, not mixed with payment logic. Enforce across all generated and existing code.

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
### API versioning
MUST use URL path versioning (e.g., /api/v1/users). NEVER use query parameters or headers for major versions. Include version in all endpoint documentation.

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
### Unit test coverage
MUST achieve ≥80% code coverage before merge. Test all public functions, error paths, and edge cases. Example: for `parse()`, test valid input, null/invalid input, and boundary conditions.

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
### Structured error handling
MUST use try-catch blocks with specific error types, log errors with context, and return structured responses. NEVER swallow exceptions silently. Example: catch(SpecificError) { logger.error({code, message, context}); return {success: false, error}} instead of generic catch-all.

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
### Structured logging
MUST use structured logging with JSON format including timestamp, level, context, and message fields. NEVER log raw strings. Example: {"timestamp": "2024-01-15T10:30:00Z", "level": "error", "context": "auth", "message": "login failed"}

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
### Input validation on every endpoint
MUST validate all inputs against schema (type, length, range, format) before processing. NEVER process unvalidated user data. Use: schema validators (zod, joi) or explicit type checks with rejection on mismatch.
## Knowledge & Context

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
### Domain vocabulary
MUST use domain-specific terms consistently across all code, docs, and AI output. Example: use 'customer' not 'user', 'transaction' not 'payment'. Enforce via code review and linting rules.

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
### Onboarding guide
MUST follow the project onboarding guide for all code and AI-generated output. Verify setup steps, dependencies, and environment config match guide requirements before committing.

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
### Decision records
MUST create ADR (Architecture Decision Record) files for all significant technical decisions. File as docs/adr/NNNN-title.md with context, decision, and consequences. Example: docs/adr/0001-use-postgres.md explaining why PostgreSQL was chosen over MySQL.

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
### Temporal context
ALWAYS include ISO 8601 timestamps (YYYY-MM-DDTHH:mm:ssZ) for time-sensitive operations, logs, and generated artifacts. Example: event_logged_at='2024-01-15T09:30:45Z' not 'Jan 15'. NEVER use relative time descriptions (e.g., 'yesterday', 'last week').

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
### Workflow documentation
MUST document all workflows with: purpose, inputs, outputs, and decision logic. Use flowchart syntax or step-by-step format. Example: '1. Validate input → 2. Process → 3. Return result with status code.' Never generate undocumented conditional branches.

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
### System architecture map
MUST maintain and reference the system architecture map in all design decisions and code generation. Include architecture context in prompts; use diagrams or docs/architecture.md as reference. NEVER add components without mapping them to existing architecture layers.
